subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
From the provided rule text and setup diagram, gameplay uses these core components: 1 Wavelength device (the large plastic unit containing the rotating wheel, hidden target, blue screen, and red dial), 1 deck of Wavelength cards (double-sided concept pairs), 2 team scoring tokens (Left Brain and Right Brain heads), and 1 guessing token for the non-Psychic team’s LEFT/RIGHT guess.

Exact quantities explicitly shown in setup text: 3 tokens total are inserted into slots (2 scoring heads + 1 guessing piece), and 1 device is placed in the center tray slot. The card deck is used as a shared draw pile, one card drawn each round in competitive mode.

Note on card count: the excerpt identifies the card deck and how it is used, but does not provide a numeric card total in the visible text. For table setup and play, use the full included deck as printed in your edition.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
1) Place the Wavelength device into the slot in the middle of the box tray.
2) Insert the two team scoring head tokens into their score tracks/slots.
3) Insert the non-Psychic team guessing token into its designated slot location.
4) Place the Wavelength card deck nearby as the draw pile.
5) Ensure the active Psychic can rotate and open/close the blue screen privately when needed.

The game is designed to be played in the box without fully removing all parts; pass/rotate the box so the active team can interact with the dial during its Team Phase.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
Divide players into 2 teams of roughly equal size. Choose one Psychic (clue giver) on each team for the first cycle.

Set initial scores as specified by rules: team going first starts at 0 points, team going second starts at 1 point. Teams may use default names Left Brain and Right Brain or rename themselves.

Only the active team’s current Psychic may view the hidden target before clueing. After clue delivery, that Psychic cannot communicate further for the round (no clarifications, no facial hints, no gestures).

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
Choose which team takes the first round. The first active Psychic begins the Psychic Phase using official order:
1) Close screen completely.
2) Draw 1 Wavelength card and choose one side (normal or weird), place it in the slot, and read the two endpoint concepts aloud.
3) Randomize hidden target by spinning the wheel while screen remains closed.
4) Open screen fully, privately inspect target center location.
5) Give one clue positioned conceptually on that spectrum.
6) Close screen and rotate the box toward the Psychic’s team.

After that clue, Team Phase starts immediately with teammates discussing where to set the dial.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Each round follows 4 phases in strict order:

1. Psychic Phase
   - Active team Psychic secretly sets and reads the spectrum card, randomizes target, views it, gives one clue, then stops communicating entirely.

2. Team Phase
   - Psychic’s teammates discuss and turn the red dial to the position they believe is closest to target center.

3. Left/Right Phase
   - Opposing team briefly decides whether actual target center is LEFT or RIGHT of the active team’s final dial position and places guessing token accordingly.

4. Scoring Phase
   - Psychic reveals target by opening screen; active team scores wedge points (2-4) if in target area; opposing team may score 1 for correct left/right guess (unless perfect center wedge was hit).

Then next round normally passes to the other team, except catch-up rule can grant consecutive turns to a trailing team that just scored 4 points and remains behind.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Psychic actions:
- Draw exactly 1 Wavelength card each round and choose one side.
- Randomize target with screen closed (no intentional target selection).
- Open fully to inspect target position.
- Give one clue ON THE SPECTRUM between card endpoints.
- Immediately stop all communication after clue.

Team actions (active team):
- Discuss freely in any style while deciding dial placement.
- Any teammate may turn dial; team decides how to negotiate control.
- Team confirms a final dial position to opponents.
- Rule-spirit guidance: avoid percentages/numeric shorthand for position (e.g., '25%').

Opposing team actions:
- Hold a short binary discussion only: is center wedge left or right of dial?
- Place guessing marker in LEFT or RIGHT slot.

Special edge actions and corrections:
- If target center wedge is not visible at all after randomization, close and spin again.
- If center wedge is partially visible at extreme edge, that is valid.
- If Psychic accidentally leaks target after clue (verbally or nonverbally), table decides penalty/reset by consensus; rulebook suggests generosity and often a reset with new card/target.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Catch-up rule (critical):
If active Psychic team scores 4 points in a round and is STILL losing after that round’s scoring, they immediately take another turn with a different Psychic. This can happen repeatedly and is designed to keep blowouts reversible.

Clue-giving firm rules:
1) Convey a single thought; avoid multi-part combo clues.
2) Don’t invent custom one-off fictional constructs just for this clue.
3) Stay on topic to the spectrum meanings (avoid unrelated double-meaning exploits).
4) Don’t use card words themselves or synonyms from the same word family.
5) No numbers/percentages/ratios as positional code (numbers in proper names are allowed).

Clue-giving suggestions (recommended for better fun):
- Be concise (often five words or fewer).
- Avoid modifiers ('very', 'almost', 'kind of') that split interpretation into two clues.
- Prefer concrete proper nouns when category prompts encourage specific exemplars.

Geometry note from FAQ:
When extreme target placement reveals tiny opposite-side color slivers, teams should ignore those and aim based on the side containing the center wedge (4-point target center).

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
Round scoring:
- Active (Psychic) team scores the value of wedge where their dial lands inside target area: 2, 3, or 4 points.
- If dial sits exactly on an edge between two active-team wedge outcomes, active team gets the better result.
- Opposing team scores 1 point if they correctly guessed LEFT/RIGHT relative to target center.
- Exception: if active team hit perfect 4-point center wedge, opposing team cannot score left/right bonus.

Game end trigger:
Once a team reaches 10 points, game ends immediately and higher score wins.

Tie handling:
If tied when game would end, both teams take a sudden-death turn; team that scores more points in that round wins (including any left/right bonus). If still tied, repeat sudden-death rounds until a winner is determined.

All scoring sources:
1) Active team target wedge points (2-4).
2) Opponent left/right guess bonus (1, conditionally denied on perfect center hit).

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Set a team calibration habit in first rounds.
Before touching the dial, quickly anchor both spectrum endpoints with one obvious example each. Then place the clue relative to those anchors. This prevents overreacting to one person’s intuition.

2) Psychic: choose clues your team shares context for.
A brilliant clue with private references is worse than a simple clue everyone understands. In early game, prioritize shared cultural anchors over cleverness.

3) Guessing team: decide dial ownership protocol.
Because anyone can turn the dial, chaotic 'grab wars' cost precision. Use a simple flow: open discussion -> one person final adjustment -> team lock.

4) Opposing team: exploit language mismatch, not your own bias.
When making LEFT/RIGHT guess, think 'What did this Psychic likely mean to THEIR team?' rather than where you personally place the clue.

5) Rotate Psychics often and intentionally.
Different clue styles can be stronger on different spectra; changing Psychics each round (as intended) keeps team signal quality high and avoids predictable patterns.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Multi-part clues that violate single-thought spirit.
These force teammates to weight two different ideas and usually create noisy guesses. Keep clues atomic.

2) Psychic keeps talking after clue.
Even harmless clarification leaks huge positional info. Rule is strict: after clue, total silence and straight face.

3) Teams using number language to micro-position dial.
It may feel efficient, but it bypasses intended social deduction and can make rounds flat. Use relative language ('a bit right', 'much more left').

4) Overfitting to extremes.
Players often drag dial too far toward endpoint examples and miss that many targets are only slightly off-center.

5) Opposing team overthinks LEFT/RIGHT as if full clue-solving.
That phase is binary; spending too long usually decreases accuracy. Make a quick, best-read directional call.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Decision 1: Precision clue vs robust clue
A very specific clue can target center tightly but risks complete team mismatch; a broader clue is safer but may cap score at 2-3. Choose based on team calibration confidence.

Decision 2: Early lock vs extended discussion
Long discussion can improve placement, but too much debate introduces anchoring and drift. Teams should set a soft time limit: discuss core interpretation, then commit.

Decision 3: Dial to maximize your score vs deny opponent bonus
Sometimes nudging toward guaranteed wedge points is better than chasing perfect center and risking miss; other times aiming for 4 is worth it because it also denies opponent LEFT/RIGHT bonus if hit.

Decision 4: Rule-strict vs house-flex clue culture
The firm rules prevent abuse, but groups may adapt some edges. Decide table strictness early so clue disputes do not interrupt momentum.

metadata.notes: Written from provided Wavelength rulebook excerpt as primary source. Component count for cards is not numerically visible in excerpt; used full-deck phrasing per provided text.
